turns.content: I'm looking at a new project and trying to implement Display for my custom type, but I keep writing out all the boilerplate code manually. Is there a crate that can help me automate this?

Yes, you are likely thinking of the derive_more crate which is designed to simplify trait implementations like Display and From. It reduces boilerplate significantly by allowing you to use a single macro instead of deriving each trait individually.
That sounds perfect, but I noticed some documentation mentions it can also handle things like Deref or Index. Can derive_more actually derive those traits automatically?

Yes, derive_more supports deriving many traits including Display and From, as well as Deref and Index to reduce boilerplate further. However, I want to clarify that while it can handle these specific traits, if you need to implement Into or Error, those are not automatically covered by the standard derive macros in derive_more.

Hey everyone, I'm new to this job and just built a quick proof-of-concept for our internal logging. I was thinking about using derive_more instead of manually implementing Display and FromStr for my structs because it feels like it should be simpler than the boilerplate I wrote last week with serde_json::Value.

That's a great instinct. The derive_more crate does exactly what you described by automatically implementing traits like Display and FromStr for your types, so you avoid writing the manual logic yourself. It reduces boilerplate significantly compared to manually implementing those methods.
Wait, I think I misunderstood something though. I thought derive_more only added traits like Display and FromStr but not things like Into or Error. Can you clarify what else it actually generates?

You are right to be cautious there. derive_more actually supports more than just Display and FromStr; it can also generate implementations for traits like Into, Error, Index, Deref, and many others depending on how you configure the macro. So yes, it does handle Into and Error as well if that's what your proof-of-concept needs.
